APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML conference scope: it proposes a novel, efficiency-oriented A* CCG parser that jointly models supertags and dependencies, evaluates on standard English and Japanese CCGbank benchmarks, and reports state-of-the-art gains against appropriate baselines. The methodology (bi-LSTM encoders, biaffine scoring, tri-training) and evaluation metrics (labeled/unlabeled F1, UAS) match community expectations, and the work addresses an audience interested in structured parsing, efficient inference, and semi-supervised techniques. From an experimental-reproducibility standpoint the paper appears to follow venue norms (datasets, baselines, hyperparameters reported), so I view it as a strong fit for a top NLP/ML venue. | B(5): This paper is an excellent fit for a top-tier NLP/ML venue: it proposes a clearly-motivated modeling advance (jointly factoring CCG supertags and dependencies with shared BiLSTMs and biaffine scoring) that yields state-of-the-art results on English and Japanese CCGbank and uses standard metrics and strong baselines. The work addresses core parsing and grammar-formalism audiences, uses appropriate datasets and evaluation protocols, and situates itself against relevant prior art (supertag-factored A* parsing, biaffine dependency models, tri-training), making it highly relevant to the conference community.

The methodology (bi-LSTM encoders, biaffine scoring, tri-training) and evaluation metrics (labeled/unlabeled F1, UAS) match community expectations, and the work addresses an audience interested in structured parsing, efficient inference, and semi-supervised techniques. From an experimental-reproducibility standpoint the paper appears to follow venue norms (datasets, baselines, hyperparameters reported), so I view it as a strong fit for a top NLP/ML venue.

The work addresses core parsing and grammar-formalism audiences, uses appropriate datasets and evaluation protocols, and situates itself against relevant prior art (supertag-factored A* parsing, biaffine dependency models, tri-training), making it highly relevant to the conference community.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against a strong and relevant set of baselines (supertag-factored EasySRL/Lewis et al. 2016, neuralccg/Lee et al. 2016, C&C with LSTM/Vaswani et al. 2016, Jigg shift-reduce, RBGParser, Dyer et al. 2015) on the same CCGbank datasets using standard labeled/unlabeled F1 and UAS metrics. Comparisons appear fair in dataset and metrics, and the authors explicitly discuss when prior heuristics (attach-low) and implementation differences limit performance; they also analyze how joint dependency modeling and tri‑training drive gains. However, some evaluation aspects are underreported or could be stronger for reproducibility and efficiency claims (no code mentioned, their EasySRL reimplementation is slower than originals), so while thorough, the empirical comparison has minor omissions preventing a full 5. | B(5): The paper compares against a comprehensive set of recent and relevant baselines (supertag-factored Lewis et al. 2016/EasySRL, neuralccg Lee et al. 2016, Vaswani et al. 2016, Noji & Miyao 2016, RBGParser, Dyer et al. 2015) on the same CCGbank datasets using standard labeled/unlabeled F1 and UAS metrics. Comparisons appear fair (same data and metrics) and the manuscript acknowledges limitations and when prior heuristics or models perform similarly, while clearly analyzing how the joint dependency+supertag factoring differs from and improves upon prior approaches; related work coverage is described as thorough. Overall the experimental comparisons are comprehensive and appropriately situated within prior CCG/dependency parsing work.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a strong and relevant set of baselines (supertag-factored EasySRL/Lewis et al. 2016, neuralccg/Lee et al. 2016, C&C with LSTM/Vaswani et al. 2016, Jigg shift-reduce, RBGParser, Dyer et al. 2015) on the same CCGbank datasets using standard labeled/unlabeled F1 and UAS metrics. Comparisons appear fair in dataset and metrics, and the authors explicitly discuss when prior heuristics (attach-low) and implementation differences limit performance; they also analyze how joint dependency modeling and tri‑training drive gains. However, some evaluation aspects are underreported or could be stronger for reproducibility and efficiency claims (no code mentioned, their EasySRL reimplementation is slower than originals), so while thorough, the empirical comparison has minor omissions preventing a full 5.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a comprehensive set of recent and relevant baselines (supertag-factored Lewis et al. 2016/EasySRL, neuralccg Lee et al. 2016, Vaswani et al. 2016, Noji & Miyao 2016, RBGParser, Dyer et al. 2015) on the same CCGbank datasets using standard labeled/unlabeled F1 and UAS metrics. Comparisons appear fair (same data and metrics) and the manuscript acknowledges limitations and when prior heuristics or models perform similarly, while clearly analyzing how the joint dependency+supertag factoring differs from and improves upon prior approaches; related work coverage is described as thorough. Overall the experimental comparisons are comprehensive and appropriately situated within prior CCG/dependency parsing work.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early and tracked through method and experiments; writing quality is described as good and related work coverage is thorough. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, which aids reproducibility-oriented readers like me. Figures/tables are implied effective, but a few presentation details (e.g., clearer exposition of the conversion limitations between CCGbank trees and dependencies and explicit mention of code/artifacts) could improve clarity for experimental replication. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are clearly presented early (joint A* CCG model, locally-factored predictions, tri-training extension). Notation and methodology are described clearly enough to understand how bi-LSTM encodings feed MLP/biaffine predictors and enable A* precomputation, and related work coverage is thorough. Figures/tables and examples appear informative and the writing quality is good, though a few implementation- and corpus-conversion caveats (reimplementation speed and unconvertible CCGbank trees) could be explained more precisely for full reproducibility. From a presentation standpoint this reads as a strong paper with only minor clarity/implementation-detail gaps.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early and tracked through method and experiments; writing quality is described as good and related work coverage is thorough. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, which aids reproducibility-oriented readers like me. Figures/tables are implied effective, but a few presentation details (e.g., clearer exposition of the conversion limitations between CCGbank trees and dependencies and explicit mention of code/artifacts) could improve clarity for experimental replication.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are clearly presented early (joint A* CCG model, locally-factored predictions, tri-training extension). Notation and methodology are described clearly enough to understand how bi-LSTM encodings feed MLP/biaffine predictors and enable A* precomputation, and related work coverage is thorough. Figures/tables and examples appear informative and the writing quality is good, though a few implementation- and corpus-conversion caveats (reimplementation speed and unconvertible CCGbank trees) could be explained more precisely for full reproducibility. From a presentation standpoint this reads as a strong paper with only minor clarity/implementation-detail gaps.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important practical problem—efficient, high-accuracy CCG parsing—by proposing a novel locally-factored joint model of supertags and dependencies that permits precomputation and fast A* search, and it reports state-of-the-art gains on both English and Japanese CCGbanks. The extension to tri‑training with unlabeled data and the explicit dependency modeling (especially helping Japanese) increase its practical relevance. From an experimental-methodology perspective the contributions are likely to change how CCG parsers are designed and be built upon, though the absence of mentioned released code and reported implementation/efficiency issues temper its immediate transformative potential. | B(4): This paper tackles an important problem—combining supertagging and explicit dependency modeling within an A* CCG parser—by showing a locally-factored joint model that permits precomputation and efficient search while improving accuracy. Achieving state-of-the-art results on English and substantial gains on Japanese, and demonstrating how tri-training can further boost a dependency-aware model, makes the approach likely to influence both CCG and dependency-parsing practice. The method opens clear research directions (dependency-aware A* parsing, semi-supervised learning for joint models) and has practical relevance for multilingual parsing and downstream semantics, though engineering/coverage limitations noted temper the transformative claim.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—efficient, high-accuracy CCG parsing—by proposing a novel locally-factored joint model of supertags and dependencies that permits precomputation and fast A* search, and it reports state-of-the-art gains on both English and Japanese CCGbanks. The extension to tri‑training with unlabeled data and the explicit dependency modeling (especially helping Japanese) increase its practical relevance. From an experimental-methodology perspective the contributions are likely to change how CCG parsers are designed and be built upon, though the absence of mentioned released code and reported implementation/efficiency issues temper its immediate transformative potential.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important problem—combining supertagging and explicit dependency modeling within an A* CCG parser—by showing a locally-factored joint model that permits precomputation and efficient search while improving accuracy. Achieving state-of-the-art results on English and substantial gains on Japanese, and demonstrating how tri-training can further boost a dependency-aware model, makes the approach likely to influence both CCG and dependency-parsing practice. The method opens clear research directions (dependency-aware A* parsing, semi-supervised learning for joint models) and has practical relevance for multilingual parsing and downstream semantics, though engineering/coverage limitations noted temper the transformative claim.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents broad experimental comparisons against many strong baselines on both English and Japanese CCGbanks and includes a tri‑training extension and reported hyperparameters, supporting its main modeling claims. However, important reproducibility details (e.g., code/checkpoints, random seeds, compute) are not mentioned and the claimed efficiency gains from A* are not fully measured (implementation is actually slower and left unoptimized). Ablation evidence is present (joint modeling vs. supertag‑only and tri‑training impact) but the analysis of speed/engineering factors and some conversion mismatches could be deeper. Overall the empirical work is solid with minor gaps that prevent full reproducibility and a complete assessment of efficiency claims. | B(4): The experimental evaluation is strong: cross-lingual tests on English and Japanese CCGbanks, comparisons to relevant baselines (EasySRL, neuralccg, C&C, shift-reduce/Jigg, RBGParser, Dyer et al.), and a semi-supervised tri‑training extension that yields further gains. The paper reports hyperparameters and discusses concrete limitations (conversion mismatches, slower reimplementation, and failure of heuristic attachments in Japanese), and situates itself well in prior A* CCG and dependency work. However, the summary does not mention systematic ablation/sensitivity analyses of model components (e.g., effect of joint dependency vs. supertagging alone beyond tri‑training) nor code release for exact reproducibility, so there are minor gaps in analysis and reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents broad experimental comparisons against many strong baselines on both English and Japanese CCGbanks and includes a tri‑training extension and reported hyperparameters, supporting its main modeling claims. However, important reproducibility details (e.g., code/checkpoints, random seeds, compute) are not mentioned and the claimed efficiency gains from A* are not fully measured (implementation is actually slower and left unoptimized). Ablation evidence is present (joint modeling vs. supertag‑only and tri‑training impact) but the analysis of speed/engineering factors and some conversion mismatches could be deeper. Overall the empirical work is solid with minor gaps that prevent full reproducibility and a complete assessment of efficiency claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The experimental evaluation is strong: cross-lingual tests on English and Japanese CCGbanks, comparisons to relevant baselines (EasySRL, neuralccg, C&C, shift-reduce/Jigg, RBGParser, Dyer et al.), and a semi-supervised tri‑training extension that yields further gains. The paper reports hyperparameters and discusses concrete limitations (conversion mismatches, slower reimplementation, and failure of heuristic attachments in Japanese), and situates itself well in prior A* CCG and dependency work. However, the summary does not mention systematic ablation/sensitivity analyses of model components (e.g., effect of joint dependency vs. supertagging alone beyond tri‑training) nor code release for exact reproducibility, so there are minor gaps in analysis and reproducibility.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's core claims (jointly factoring supertags and dependencies, enabling precomputation for A* search, and improved English/Japanese CCG results) are backed by experiments against a wide set of relevant baselines and reported hyperparameters. The modeling and derivations (biaffine/bilinear predictors on shared BiLSTM representations) are standard and appear correct from the summary. However, reproducibility and fairness concerns remain: code is not reported available, the authors note their EasySRL reimplementation is slower than prior work, and some CCGbank conversions are imperfect—plus no mention of statistical significance testing—so there are minor gaps that prevent a perfect technical evaluation. Overall the evidence supports the main conclusions but with these modest methodological shortcomings. | B(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and empirically supported by improvements on English and Japanese CCGbanks and by the tri-training gains; the modeling approach (jointly factoring supertag and dependency probabilities with BiLSTM and biaffine scores) is plausible and aligns with prior graph-based techniques enabling precomputation for A* search. Mathematical framing appears correct for a locally-factored model and there are no obvious derivation errors in the summary; experimental methodology uses appropriate baselines and languages. Minor concerns remain: some corpus-to-dependency conversion mismatches and a reported slower reimplementation suggest engineering/coverage issues, and the summary does not mention statistical significance tests, so a small gap in evaluation rigor exists. Overall, these are minor and do not undermine the central technical contribution.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's core claims (jointly factoring supertags and dependencies, enabling precomputation for A* search, and improved English/Japanese CCG results) are backed by experiments against a wide set of relevant baselines and reported hyperparameters. The modeling and derivations (biaffine/bilinear predictors on shared BiLSTM representations) are standard and appear correct from the summary. However, reproducibility and fairness concerns remain: code is not reported available, the authors note their EasySRL reimplementation is slower than prior work, and some CCGbank conversions are imperfect—plus no mention of statistical significance testing—so there are minor gaps that prevent a perfect technical evaluation. Overall the evidence supports the main conclusions but with these modest methodological shortcomings.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and empirically supported by improvements on English and Japanese CCGbanks and by the tri-training gains; the modeling approach (jointly factoring supertag and dependency probabilities with BiLSTM and biaffine scores) is plausible and aligns with prior graph-based techniques enabling precomputation for A* search. Mathematical framing appears correct for a locally-factored model and there are no obvious derivation errors in the summary; experimental methodology uses appropriate baselines and languages. Minor concerns remain: some corpus-to-dependency conversion mismatches and a reported slower reimplementation suggest engineering/coverage issues, and the summary does not mention statistical significance tests, so a small gap in evaluation rigor exists. Overall, these are minor and do not undermine the central technical contribution.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper brings a notable and practical novelty by jointly factoring CCG supertagging and dependency prediction into a locally-factored model that permits full precomputation and efficient A* search — an idea that meaningfully departs from purely supertag-factored parsers (Lewis et al. 2016) and from separate dependency or transition-based approaches. The use of shared bi-LSTM representations with biaffine scoring builds on known components (Dozat & Manning style), but combining them to enable exact A* decoding and integrating dependency-aware tri-training for semi-supervised gains is a non-trivial synthesis that clearly differentiates the contribution. From an expert’s perspective this would be an informative advance rather than a mere incremental engineering tweak, though it reuses several established modeling primitives. | B(4): The paper presents a notable and plausible novelty by jointly factoring CCG supertags and dependency attachments within a locally-factored model that enables precomputation for true A* search—this contrasts clearly with prior supertag-only A* work (e.g., Lewis et al., 2016) and with separate biaffine dependency models. The combination of shared BiLSTM representations, biaffine head prediction, and an A*-compatible factorization is a non-trivial engineering and modeling extension beyond existing components (Dozat & Manning style dependency scoring + supertagging). Tri-training to inject unlabeled data into this dependency-aware CCG model is a useful, if expected, augmentation. An expert would find the joint A* formulation informative and sufficiently differentiated from prior single-factor approaches, though many constituent techniques are themselves established.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper brings a notable and practical novelty by jointly factoring CCG supertagging and dependency prediction into a locally-factored model that permits full precomputation and efficient A* search — an idea that meaningfully departs from purely supertag-factored parsers (Lewis et al. 2016) and from separate dependency or transition-based approaches. The use of shared bi-LSTM representations with biaffine scoring builds on known components (Dozat & Manning style), but combining them to enable exact A* decoding and integrating dependency-aware tri-training for semi-supervised gains is a non-trivial synthesis that clearly differentiates the contribution. From an expert’s perspective this would be an informative advance rather than a mere incremental engineering tweak, though it reuses several established modeling primitives.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a notable and plausible novelty by jointly factoring CCG supertags and dependency attachments within a locally-factored model that enables precomputation for true A* search—this contrasts clearly with prior supertag-only A* work (e.g., Lewis et al., 2016) and with separate biaffine dependency models. The combination of shared BiLSTM representations, biaffine head prediction, and an A*-compatible factorization is a non-trivial engineering and modeling extension beyond existing components (Dozat & Manning style dependency scoring + supertagging). Tri-training to inject unlabeled data into this dependency-aware CCG model is a useful, if expected, augmentation. An expert would find the joint A* formulation informative and sufficiently differentiated from prior single-factor approaches, though many constituent techniques are themselves established.

main_claims: Proposes a new A* CCG parsing model that jointly factors the probability of a CCG tree into CCG categories (supertags) and dependency structure, both predicted by bi-directional LSTMs.
The joint, locally-factored model permits precomputation of probabilities and efficient A* search while explicitly modeling sentence structure via dependencies.
The model attains state-of-the-art results on English and Japanese CCG parsing (reported improvements in labeled and unlabeled F1 on English CCGbank and strong gains on Japanese CCGbank).
Extends tri-training to learn the dependency-aware model from unlabeled data, providing further performance boosts.
_summary.methodology: They use shared bi-LSTM hidden vectors to predict both P(tag) and P(dep) with MLPs and biaffine/bilinear transformations (Dozat & Manning style); the model is locally factored so all per-word probabilities (supertag distributions and head distributions) can be precomputed for efficient A* parsing.
baselines: C&C with LSTM supertagger (Vaswani et al., 2016)
Shift-reduce CCG parser in Jigg (Noji and Miyao, 2016)
metrics: Labeled F1 (of extracted CCG semantic dependencies)
Unlabeled F1 (of extracted CCG semantic dependencies)
limitations: Parser implementation (EasySRL reimplementation) is slower than the original supertag-factored implementation, indicating room for optimizing efficiency.
Some annotated CCG trees in CCGbank cannot be converted to dependencies due to mismatch between the parser's rule set and the corpus grammar.
Deterministic heuristics used by prior work (e.g., attach-low) are not flawless, especially in languages like Japanese (motivating dependency modeling).

reviewer_a.name: Experimental Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer and reproducibility researcher with 8+ years building and evaluating neural NLP systems, focusing on parsing and structured prediction implementations.
traits: insists on clear descriptions of model training details (optimizer, hyperparameters, seeds, compute) and will notice omitted settings
values thorough ablations that isolate contributions (e.g., effect of joint modeling vs. stronger encoders, tri-training impact, choice of dependency head representation)
checks for proper and fair baselines, identical preprocessing/tokenization, and significance testing of reported improvements
looks for reproducibility artifacts (code, precomputed scores, model checkpoints) and for whether claimed efficiency (A* speedups) is measured and compared
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Evaluates papers by tracing claims to experimental evidence, demanding controlled comparisons and artifacts sufficient to reproduce the results.

reviewer_b.name: Senior CCG/Parsing Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Professor-level researcher with 15+ years studying CCG, dependency-formalisms, and parsing evaluation, author of several works on grammar formalisms and parsing algorithms.
traits: assesses novelty relative to prior CCG and dependency-parsing work and how the joint modeling advances linguistic or algorithmic understanding
values the clarity of the modeling assumptions (what is factored, when global constraints are enforced) and how they impact linguistic interpretability
notices broader impact: applicability to other languages, utility for downstream tasks, and whether the approach meaningfully changes best practices in parsing

evaluates how well the paper situates itself in related work (A* CCG parsing history, tri-training/ semi-supervised methods, biaffine dependency models) and highlights missing citations or connections
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Judges the paper on novelty, conceptual contribution, and relevance to the parsing community, emphasizing whether the work meaningfully moves the field forward.
